examples.title: FIFO Page Replacement Algorithm
examples.description: Implements the First-In-First-Out page replacement algorithm used in virtual memory systems
examples.code:
ALGORITHM FIFO-Page-Replace
INPUT: Reference string R[], number of frames F
BEGIN
  SET frames[F] = empty
  SET pageQueue = empty queue
  SET pageFaults = 0

  FOR EACH page p IN R[] DO
    IF p NOT IN frames THEN
      IF frames are full THEN
        oldPage = pageQueue.dequeue()
        frames[oldPage] = p
      ELSE
        frames[nextFree] = p
      ENDIF
      pageQueue.enqueue(p)
      pageFaults = pageFaults + 1
    ENDIF
  ENDFOR

  RETURN pageFaults
END
examples.explanation: This algorithm maintains a queue of pages in memory. When a page fault occurs and all frames are full, the oldest page (first to enter) is replaced. Simple to implement but doesn't consider page usage patterns.
examples.timeComplexity: O(n) where n is number of page references
examples.spaceComplexity: O(f) where f is number of frames

examples.title: LRU Page Replacement Algorithm
examples.description: Implements Least Recently Used page replacement using counters
examples.code:
ALGORITHM LRU-Page-Replace
INPUT: Reference string R[], number of frames F
BEGIN
  SET frames[F] = empty
  SET counter = 0
  SET lastUsed[F] = 0
  SET pageFaults = 0

  FOR EACH page p IN R[] DO
    counter = counter + 1
    IF p IN frames THEN
      lastUsed[frameIndexOf(p)] = counter
    ELSE
      IF frames are full THEN
        lruFrame = index with minimum lastUsed[]
        frames[lruFrame] = p
        lastUsed[lruFrame] = counter
      ELSE
        frames[nextFree] = p
        lastUsed[nextFree] = counter
      ENDIF
      pageFaults = pageFaults + 1
    ENDIF
  ENDFOR

  RETURN pageFaults
END
examples.explanation: LRU tracks when each page was last used. On replacement, it evicts the page with the oldest lastUsed timestamp. Better than FIFO as it considers actual page usage but requires hardware support for accuracy.
examples.timeComplexity: O(n*f) with naive implementation, O(n) with better data structures

examples.title: Address Translation Simulation
examples.description: Demonstrates virtual to physical address translation in a paged memory system
examples.code:
class MemoryManager:
    def __init__(self, page_size, num_frames):
        self.page_size = page_size  # bytes
        self.num_frames = num_frames
        self.frame_size = page_size
        # Page table: page_number -> (frame_number, valid)
        self.page_table = {}
        # Free frames list
        self.free_frames = list(range(num_frames))
        
    def allocate_page(self, virtual_page):
        """Allocate a physical frame to virtual page"""
        if not self.free_frames:
            return False  # No free frames
        frame = self.free_frames.pop(0)
        self.page_table[virtual_page] = {'frame': frame, 'valid': True}
        return True
    
    def translate_address(self, virtual_addr):
        """Convert virtual address to physical address"""
        page_num = virtual_addr // self.page_size
        offset = virtual_addr % self.page_size
        
        if page_num not in self.page_table:
            raise PageFaultException(f"Page {page_num} not in memory")
            
        frame_num = self.page_table[page_num]['frame']
        physical_addr = frame_num * self.page_size + offset
        
        return physical_addr

# Example usage
mm = MemoryManager(page_size=1024, num_frames=8)
mm.allocate_page(0)  # Allocate frame 0 to virtual page 0
mm.allocate_page(2)  # Allocate frame 1 to virtual page 2

virtual_addr = 2500  # Virtual address
physical_addr = mm.translate_address(virtual_addr)
print(f"Virtual: {virtual_addr} -> Physical: {physical_addr}")
# Page 2 (starts at 2048), offset = 2500-2048=452
# Frame 1: physical = 1*1024 + 452 = 1476
examples.explanation: This simulation demonstrates how paging works: dividing virtual address into page number and offset, looking up the frame in page table, and combining frame number with offset to get physical address.
examples.timeComplexity: O(1) for address translation with valid page table
examples.spaceComplexity: O(p) where p is number of allocated pages + O(f) for frames

examples.title: Contiguous Memory Allocation (First-Fit)
examples.description: Implements first-fit memory allocation in contiguous allocation system
examples.explanation: This demonstrates contiguous memory allocation using first-fit strategy. The memory is a single array that gets split when processes are allocated and merged when freed. Shows internal fragmentation (unused space in allocated block) and potential external fragmentation.
examples.timeComplexity: O(n) where n is number of memory blocks
